devanagari: नारद उवाच । सैनिका भयनाम्नो ये बर्हिष्मन् दिन्दिष्टकारिणः । प्रज्वारकालकन्याभ्यां विचेरुः अवनीमिमाम् ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: nārada uvāca sainikā bhayanāmno ye barhiṣman dindiṣṭakāriṇaḥ prajvārakālakanyābhyāṃ viceruḥ avanīmimām

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
Nārada said:
Oh King Barhiṣman (Prācīnabarhis)! Those soldiers of king Bhaya (i.e. diseases) who were the agents of fate, roamed over this earth accompanied by Prajvāra and the daughter of Kāla.

devanagari: ते एकदा तु रभसा पुरञ्जनपुरीं नृप । रुरुधुर्भौमभोगाढ्यां जरत्पन्नगपालिताम् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: te ekadā tu rabhasā purañjanapurīṃ nṛpa rurudhurbhaumabhogāḍhyāṃ jaratpannagapālitām

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ jṛ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ jṛ class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ jṛ class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ jṝ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ jṝ class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ jṝ class 1 verb]

translation: And all of a sudden, they swooped on the city of Purañjana on the D-day (on a certain day) and besieged the city, rich in earthly enjoyments, and guarded by the old serpent (i.e. the Prāṇa in old age whose physical activities became sluggish).

devanagari: कालकन्यापि बुभुजे पुरञ्जनपुरं बलात् । ययाभिभूतः पुरुषः सद्यो निःसारतामियात् ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: kālakanyāpi bubhuje purañjanapuraṃ balāt yayābhibhūtaḥ puruṣaḥ sadyo niḥsāratāmiyāt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]
[perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: The daughter of Kāla also enjoyed perforce the city of Purañjana. Whatever man was overpowered by her, would at once become powerless.

devanagari: तयोपभुज्यमानां वै यवनाः सर्वतोदिशम् । द्वार्भिः प्रविश्य सुभृशं प्रार्दयन् सकलां पुरीम् ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: tayopabhujyamānāṃ vai yavanāḥ sarvatodiśam dvārbhiḥ praviśya subhṛśaṃ prārdayan sakalāṃ purīm

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: The Yavanas (i.e. diseases) entered the city through all the gates from all directions and excessively ravished it, while it was being enjoyed by her (the body of the jīva is battered by repeated attacks of various diseases).

devanagari: तस्यां प्रपीड्यमानायां अभिमानी पुरञ्जनः । अवापोरुविधान् तापान् कुटुम्बी ममताकुलः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: tasyāṃ prapīḍyamānāyāṃ abhimānī purañjanaḥ avāporuvidhān tāpān kuṭumbī mamatākulaḥ
translation: The city being (thus) devastated, Purañjana who identified himself with it as his own Self, and whose mind was troubled by his sense of mine-ness for his family (viz. intelligence, the mind, sense-organs etc.) underwent agonies of various kinds.

devanagari: कन्योपगूढो नष्टश्रीः कृपणो विषयात्मकः । नष्टप्रज्ञो हृतैश्वर्यो गन्धर्वयवनैर्बलात् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: kanyopagūḍho naṣṭaśrīḥ kṛpaṇo viṣayātmakaḥ naṣṭaprajño hṛtaiśvaryo gandharvayavanairbalāt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [vocative single from √ naś class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ naś class 4 verb]
[vocative single from √ naś class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ naś class 4 verb]
[vocative single from √ naś class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ naś class 4 verb]
[vocative single from √ naś class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ naś class 4 verb]

translation: Clasped in the arms of the daughter of Kāla, bereft of his splendour and affluence, the wretched Purañjana whose mind was engrossed in sense-pleasure, lost his power of judgment and was forcibly deprived of his powers (viz. of making physical movements) by Gandharvas and Yavanas.

devanagari: विशीर्णां स्वपुरीं वीक्ष्य प्रतिकूलाननादृतान् । पुत्रान् पौत्रानुगामात्यान् जायां च गतसौहृदाम् ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: viśīrṇāṃ svapurīṃ vīkṣya pratikūlānanādṛtān putrān pautrānugāmātyān jāyāṃ ca gatasauhṛdām
translation: He saw that his capital city was completely razed and found that his sons, grandsons, attendants and ministers were antagonistic and disrespectful to him and his wife had lost love for him.

devanagari: आत्मानं कन्यया ग्रस्तं पञ्चालान् अरिदूषितान् । दुरन्तचिन्तामापन्नो न लेभे तत्प्रतिक्रियाम् ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: ātmānaṃ kanyayā grastaṃ pañcālān aridūṣitān durantacintāmāpanno na lebhe tatpratikriyām

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ gras class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ gras class 1 verb]
[perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: He found that he was in the clutches of the daughter (of Kāla) and that the Pañcālas were infested with enemies. He was lost in boundless anxiety and could not find any remedy against it.

devanagari: कामानभिलषन्दीनो यातयामांश्च कन्यया । विगतात्मगतिस्नेहः पुत्रदारांश्च लालयन् ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: kāmānabhilaṣandīno yātayāmāṃśca kanyayā vigatātmagatisnehaḥ putradārāṃśca lālayan

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ laṣ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ laṣ class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ lal ], [vocative single from √ lal ]

translation: The miserable king still hankered after the pleasures which were made stale (due to dyspepsia etc.) by the daughter of Kāla. He lost his affection for his son etc. here and his spiritual position hereafter, and continued in fondling his wife and sons.

devanagari: गन्धर्वयवनाक्रान्तां कालकन्योपमर्दिताम् । हातुं प्रचक्रमे राजा तां पुरीमनिकामतः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: gandharvayavanākrāntāṃ kālakanyopamarditām hātuṃ pracakrame rājā tāṃ purīmanikāmataḥ
translation: Very reluctantly did the king proceed to abandon the city which was over-run by Gandharvas and Yavanas and was ravaged by the daughter of Kāla.

devanagari: भयनाम्नोऽग्रजो भ्राता प्रज्वारः प्रत्युपस्थितः । ददाह तां पुरीं कृत्स्नां भ्रातुः प्रियचिकीर्षया ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: bhayanāmno dadāha tāṃ purīṃ kṛtsnāṃ bhrātuḥ priyacikīrṣayā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: (At that time) there arrived Prajvāra (i.e. the high fatal fever) the elder brother of Bhaya (the chief of Yavanas). With a desire to render service to his brother, Prajvāra burnt down the entire city. (This indicates high temperature of the body).

devanagari: तस्यां सन्दह्यमानायां सपौरः सपरिच्छदः । कौटुम्बिकः कुटुम्बिन्या उपातप्यत सान्वयः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: tasyāṃ sandahyamānāyāṃ sapauraḥ saparicchadaḥ kauṭumbikaḥ kuṭumbinyā upātapyata sānvayaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect middle third single], [imperfect passive third single]

translation: When the city was aflame, the head of the family (Purañjana) along with his wife, children, dependents and citizens was greatly tormented.

devanagari: यवनोपरुद्धायतनो ग्रस्तायां कालकन्यया । पुर्यां प्रज्वारसंसृष्टः पुरपालोऽन्वतप्यत ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: yavanoparuddhāyatano grastāyāṃ kālakanyayā puryāṃ prajvārasaṃsṛṣṭaḥ purapālo

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect middle third single], [imperfect passive third single]

translation: When the city was occupied by the daughter of Kāla and his own home was surrounded by Yavanas, the guard of the city was greatly distressed when he was attacked by Prajvāra. (When the aged man is about to die, his body is in grip of diseases and the jīva is tormented every moment.)

devanagari: न शेके सोऽवितुं तत्र पुरुकृच्छ्रोरुवेपथुः । गन्तुमैच्छत्ततो वृक्ष कोटरादिव सानलात् ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: na śeke so gantumaicchattato vṛkṣa koṭarādiva sānalāt
translation: When the serpent Prajāgara was not able to defend the city any longer there, he was violently trembling with severe agonies and he wished to escape from it (like a serpent) from the hollow of a tree ablaze with fire.

devanagari: शिथिलावयवो यर्हि गन्धर्वैर्हृतपौरुषः । यवनैररिभी राजन् उपरुद्धो रुरोद ह ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: śithilāvayavo yarhi gandharvairhṛtapauruṣaḥ yavanairaribhī rājan uparuddho ruroda ha

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: Oh King! When his limbs became weak and disabled and his prowess was thwarted by Gandharvas and he was surrounded by the inimical Yavanas, he verily wept bitterly.

devanagari: दुहितॄ पुत्रपौत्रांश्च जामिजामातृपार्षदान् । स्वत्वावशिष्टं यत्किञ्चिद् गृहकोशपरिच्छदम् ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: duhitṝ putrapautrāṃśca jāmijāmātṛpārṣadān svatvāvaśiṣṭaṃ yatkiñcid gṛhakośaparicchadam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: The householder (Purañjana) entertained a false notion about his house etc. When the time of separation from his wife arrived, he became very miserable. Possessed with egotism and a false sense of mine-ness about his daughters, sons, grandsons, daughters-in-law. sons-in-law, attendants and whatever (little) remained of his house, treasures and belongings, he thought:

devanagari: अहं ममेति स्वीकृत्य गृहेषु कुमतिर्गृही । दध्यौ प्रमदया दीनो विप्रयोग उपस्थिते ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: ahaṃ mameti svīkṛtya gṛheṣu kumatirgṛhī dadhyau pramadayā dīno viprayoga upasthite

translation: The householder (Purañjana) entertained a false notion about his house etc. When the time of separation from his wife arrived, he became very miserable. Possessed with egotism and a false sense of mine-ness about his daughters, sons, grandsons, daughters-in-law. sons-in-law, attendants and whatever (little) remained of his house, treasures and belongings, he thought:

devanagari: लोकान्तरं गतवति मय्यनाथा कुटुम्बिनी । वर्तिष्यते कथं त्वेषा बालकान् अनुशोचती ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: lokāntaraṃ gatavati mayyanāthā kuṭumbinī vartiṣyate kathaṃ tveṣā bālakān anuśocatī

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ śuc class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ śuc class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ śuc class 1 verb]

translation: “When I have departed to the next world, how will this mistress of the family, being bereaved of her lord and lamenting for the children, carry on her life?

devanagari: न मय्यनाशिते भुङ्क्ते नास्नाते स्नाति मत्परा । मयि रुष्टे सुसन्त्रस्ता भर्त्सिते यतवाग्भयात् ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: na mayyanāśite bhuṅkte nāsnāte snāti matparā mayi ruṣṭe susantrastā bhartsite yatavāgbhayāt

translation: She is so devoted to me that she does not eat food until I have taken mine; nor does she take her bath till I have bathed. She gets terribly afraid, when I am angry; when reproached by me, she keeps quiet through fear.

devanagari: प्रबोधयति माविज्ञं व्युषिते शोककर्शिता । वर्त्मैतद् गृहमेधीयं वीरसूरपि नेष्यति ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: prabodhayati māvijñaṃ vyuṣite śokakarśitā vartmaitad gṛhamedhīyaṃ vīrasūrapi neṣyati

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb], [vocative dual from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb], [accusative dual from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb], [locative single from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb]
[nominative dual from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb], [vocative single from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb], [vocative dual from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb], [accusative dual from √ vyuṣ class 10 verb]

translation: When I become thoughtless, she brings me round (lit. wakens me) to the proper path. When I proceed on a long journey, she becomes emaciated through grief. True, she is the mother of heroic children. But how will she desire to carry on the householder’s life (without me)—she will certainly die.

devanagari: कथं नु दारका दीना दारकीर्वापरायणाः । वर्तिष्यन्ते मयि गते भिन्ननाव इवोदधौ ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: kathaṃ nu dārakā dīnā dārakīrvāparāyaṇāḥ vartiṣyante mayi gate bhinnanāva ivodadhau
translation: How can my miserable sons or supportless daughters possibly live when I am gone, as they are like unto passengers on a boat broken in the midst of the sea.”

devanagari: एवं कृपणया बुद्ध्या शोचन्तमतदर्हणम् । ग्रहीतुं कृतधीरेनं भयनामाभ्यपद्यत ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ kṛpaṇayā buddhyā śocantamatadarhaṇam grahītuṃ kṛtadhīrenaṃ bhayanāmābhyapadyata
translation: While he was thus lamenting like a mean-minded fellow which was unbecoming to him (as he was really the Brahman himself, the Yavana king called Bhaya with a determination to capture him, drew near him.

devanagari: पशुवद्यवनैरेष नीयमानः स्वकं क्षयम् । अन्वद्रवन्ननुपथाः शोचन्तो भृशमातुराः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: paśuvadyavanaireṣa nīyamānaḥ svakaṃ kṣayam anvadravannanupathāḥ śocanto bhṛśamāturāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]
[nominative plural from √ śuc class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ śuc class 1 verb]

translation: [1] When he was being dragged like a beast to his (destined) place by the Yavanas, his followers being extremely grieved, over-anxious and afflicted ran after him.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
When the jīva is being taken to his next birth predetermined by his karmas, his subtle-senses etc. follow him—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā, Bhāgavata Candrikā
Cf. tam ut-krāmantam prāṇo nūtkrāmati prāṇam anūtkrāmantaṃ sarve prāṇā anūtkrā?manti—Bṛhad. Up. 4.4.2.

devanagari: पुरीं विहायोपगत उपरुद्धो भुजङ्गमः । यदा तमेवानु पुरी विशीर्णा प्रकृतिं गता ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: purīṃ vihāyopagata uparuddho bhujaṅgamaḥ yadā tamevānu purī viśīrṇā prakṛtiṃ gatā
translation: When the serpent which was blocked (by the Yavanas) escaped from the city to join (his master Purañjana) the city was completely razed and reduced to its elements.

devanagari: विकृष्यमाणः प्रसभं यवनेन बलीयसा । नाविन्दत्तमसाऽविष्टः सखायं सुहृदं पुरः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: vikṛṣyamāṇaḥ prasabhaṃ yavanena balīyasā nāvindattamasā
translation: Though Purañjana was forcibly dragged by the powerful Yavana, he was so much overpowered with tamas (ignorance) that he did not remember (Avijñāta) who was his former well-wisher and companion.

devanagari: तं यज्ञपशवोऽनेन संज्ञप्ता येऽदयालुना । कुठारैश्चिच्छिदुः क्रुद्धाः स्मरन्तोऽमीवमस्य तत् ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ yajñapaśavo kuṭhāraiścicchiduḥ kruddhāḥ smaranto

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ krudh class 4 verb], [vocative plural from √ krudh class 4 verb]
[nominative plural from √ krudh class 4 verb], [vocative plural from √ krudh class 4 verb], [accusative plural from √ krudh class 4 verb]
[nominative plural from √ smṛ class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ smṛ class 1 verb]

translation: The sacrificial beasts which were ruthlessly slaughtered by him, remembered his cruelty and getting enraged cut him down with axes (or axe-like sharp horns).

devanagari: अनन्तपारे तमसि मग्नो नष्टस्मृतिः समाः । शाश्वतीरनुभूयार्तिं प्रमदासङ्गदूषितः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: anantapāre tamasi magno naṣṭasmṛtiḥ samāḥ śāśvatīranubhūyārtiṃ pramadāsaṅgadūṣitaḥ
translation: He sank in bottomless darkness (hell). He lost his memory. Due to his sinful contacts with women, he suffered unbounded afflictions for eternal years.

devanagari: तामेव मनसा गृह्णन् बभूव प्रमदोत्तमा । अनन्तरं विदर्भस्य राजसिंहस्य वेश्मनि ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: tāmeva manasā gṛhṇan babhūva pramadottamā anantaraṃ vidarbhasya rājasiṃhasya veśmani

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ grah class 9 verb], [vocative single from √ grah class 9 verb]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]

translation: [2] As he brooded over that very (virtuous) wife at the time of death, he was born later as a most beautiful damsel, the princess of (the pious king) Rājasiṃha of Vidarbha.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā and Bhāgavata Candrikā explain that brooding over a woman at the time of death results in birth as a woman. Purañjana thought of his virtuous wife at the time of his death and due to his good merits he was born in the pious family of Rājasiṃha of Vidarbha and got married to Malayadhvaja, a devotee of Viṣṇu. Both explain the pun on Vidarbha.
This allegory is explained differently by different commentators:

devanagari: उपयेमे वीर्यपणां वैदर्भीं मलयध्वजः । युधि निर्जित्य राजन्यान् पाण्ड्यः परपुरञ्जयः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: upayeme vīryapaṇāṃ vaidarbhīṃ malayadhvajaḥ yudhi nirjitya rājanyān pāṇḍyaḥ parapurañjayaḥ
translation: [3] The Pāṇḍya king, Malayadhvaja, the conqueror of the cities of enemies, vanquished other kings in the battle and married the princess of Vidarbha who was offered as prize for bravery.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
(i) Purañjana was thus associated with a great devotee of Viṣṇu. For Malayadhvaja signifies an eminent votary in the southern country—Malaya—famous for Vaiṣṇavism. Pāṇḍya means one who deserves Paṇḍā—intellect capable of deciding the truth.—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā, Bhāgavata Candrikā
(ii) Malayadhvaja—one whose mind has attained serenity etc. by service of Hari. By means of yama, niyama he defeated and subdued his passionate senses (the inimical kings). He is Pāṇḍya—one endowed with discriminatory intelligence. Para-purañjaya—conquerors of viṣayas or objects of senses. Vaidarbhī—keen intellect. Purañjana and Purañjanī brooded over each other at the time of their death and were born as Pramadottamā and Malayadhvaja—Padaratnāvalī
(iii) Siddhāntapradīpa: Malayadhvaja—famous in Bhāratavarṣa.
Para-Purañjayaḥ [Purañjaya]—One who conquers, i.e. attains to Vaikuṇṭha.
Vīrya-paṇā [paṇām]—One available by good merits.
Jīva who was infatuated with Buddhi (Intelligence) changed one body after another, once upon a time finds a spiritual preceptor leading to Mokṣa (Liberation).

devanagari: तस्यां स जनयां चक्र आत्मजामसितेक्षणाम् । यवीयसः सप्त सुतान् सप्त द्रविडभूभृतः ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: tasyāṃ sa janayāṃ cakra ātmajāmasitekṣaṇām yavīyasaḥ sapta sutān sapta draviḍabhūbhṛtaḥ

translation: [4] He begot from her one daughter of dark-blue eyes, and seven sons younger to her who became the kings of Draviḍa countries.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
Allegory explained:
(i) Bhāvāratha Dīpikā, Bhāgavata Candrikā, Bālaprabodhini: ātmajā—Liking for the service of Lord Kṛṣṇa.
Draviḍa-bhūmi: The Draviḍa country is protected by devotion of the type of Śravaṇa etc.
In association with devotees of Viṣṇu, a desire to serve him is treated and it is followed with seven kinds of bhakti or devotion viz. Śravaṇa (study of scriptures), Kīrtana (singing Lord’s praise), Smaraṇa (remembering him—his name), Pādasevana (serving his feet) arcana (worshipping him), vandana (laying oneself prostrate before him). The Draviḍa country is famous for votaries of Viṣṇu.
(ii) Padaratnāvalī names the seven sons as follows : Virāga (renunciation), Vinaya (discipline), Dāna (charity), Dhairya (courage), Aspardhana (nonjealousy), Viveka (discrimination), Dama (self control.

devanagari: एकैकस्याभवत्तेषां राजन् अर्बुदमर्बुदम् । भोक्ष्यते यद्वंशधरैः मही मन्वन्तरं परम् ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: ekaikasyābhavatteṣāṃ rājan arbudamarbudam bhokṣyate yadvaṃśadharaiḥ mahī manvantaraṃ param

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]

translation: Oh King, each one of them had hundred million sons, whose descendants will enjoy this earth for one Manvantara and more.

devanagari: अगस्त्यः प्राग्दुहितरं उपयेमे धृतव्रताम् । यस्यां दृढच्युतो जात इध्मवाहात्मजो मुनिः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: agastyaḥ prāgduhitaraṃ upayeme dhṛtavratām yasyāṃ dṛḍhacyuto jāta idhmavāhātmajo muniḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ jan class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 3 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 4 verb]

translation: Agastya married the first-born daughter who was constant in observance of vows.[5] Through her was born Dṛḍhacyuta who had sage Idhmavāha as his son.
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Different interpretations of the allegory:
Agastya: (i) The mind (Bhāvāratha Dīpikā, VC., Bālaprabodhini, Siddhāntapradīpa) (ii) One desirous of Mokṣa Bhāgavata Candrikā (iii) A devotee of Viṣṇu (Padaratnāvalī).
Dṛḍhacyuta: (i) Devoid of any desire for even hereafter such as Satyaloka—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā
(ii) Who has absolutely no attachment for sons etc. (Padaratnāvalī).
(iii) One not attached to any pleasure here and hereafter—VC., Siddhāntapradīpa, Bālaprabodhini
Idhmavāha: (i) Desire to worship the feet of the spiritual preceptor for initiation and obtaining the Lord’s grace—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā, Bhāgavata Candrikā
(ii) Desire to be a pupil of a spiritual preceptor—Padaratnāvalī

devanagari: विभज्य तनयेभ्यः क्ष्मां राजर्षिर्मलयध्वजः । आरिराधयिषुः कृष्णं स जगाम कुलाचलम् ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: vibhajya tanayebhyaḥ kṣmāṃ rājarṣirmalayadhvajaḥ ārirādhayiṣuḥ kṛṣṇaṃ sa jagāma kulācalam

translation: The royal sage Malayaḍhvaja distributed the land, among his sons. With a desire to propitiate Lord Kṛṣṇa he retired to the Kulācala mountain, viz., Malaya.

devanagari: हित्वा गृहान् सुतान् भोगान् वैदर्भी मदिरेक्षणा । अन्वधावत पाण्ड्येशं ज्योत्स्नेव रजनीकरम् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: hitvā gṛhān sutān bhogān vaidarbhī madirekṣaṇā anvadhāvata pāṇḍyeśaṃ jyotsneva rajanīkaram

translation: The Princess of Vidarbha with bewitching eyes abandoned her palaces, sons and pleasures and ran after the Pāṇḍya king (Malayadhvaja) like the moonlight following the Moon.

devanagari: तत्र चन्द्रवसा नाम ताम्रपर्णी वटोदका । तत्पुण्यसलिलैर्नित्यं उभयत्रात्मनो मृजन् ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: tatra candravasā nāma tāmraparṇī vaṭodakā tatpuṇyasalilairnityaṃ ubhayatrātmano mṛjan

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ mṛj class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ mṛj class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ mṛj class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ mṛj class 6 verb]

translation: There flow three rivers viz., Candravasā, Tāmraparṇī and Vaṭodakā in that region. Every day he washed off both his internal (mental) and external (bodily) impurities with the holy waters of the rivers.

devanagari: कन्दाष्टिभिर्मूलफलैः पुष्पपर्णैस्तृणोदकैः । वर्तमानः शनैर्गात्र कर्शनं तप आस्थितः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: kandāṣṭibhirmūlaphalaiḥ puṣpaparṇaistṛṇodakaiḥ vartamānaḥ śanairgātra karśanaṃ tapa āsthitaḥ
translation: He performed austere penance by subsisting on bulbous roots, seeds, roots, fruits, flowers, leaves, blades of grass and water. (Thereby) his body got gradually emaciated.

devanagari: शीतोष्णवातवर्षाणि क्षुत्पिपासे प्रियाप्रिये । सुखदुःखे इति द्वन्द्वान्यजयत्समदर्शनः ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: śītoṣṇavātavarṣāṇi kṣutpipāse priyāpriye sukhaduḥkhe iti dvandvānyajayatsamadarśanaḥ
translation: He conquered the pairs of opposites like cold and heat, wind and rain, hunger and thirst, agreeable and disagreeable, pleasure and pain, viewing them with equanimity of mind.

devanagari: तपसा विद्यया पक्व कषायो नियमैर्यमैः । युयुजे ब्रह्मण्यात्मानं विजिताक्षानिलाशयः ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: tapasā vidyayā pakva kaṣāyo niyamairyamaiḥ yuyuje brahmaṇyātmānaṃ vijitākṣānilāśayaḥ

translation: He burnt down his passions by austerities and knowledge. He conquered his senses, breath (Prāṇa) and mind by observing religious vows and rules of self-discipline called Yama and Niyama. He reflected over the identity of his Soul with Brahman (lit. He united his Soul with Brahman).

devanagari: आस्ते स्थाणुरिवैकत्र दिव्यं वर्षशतं स्थिरः । वासुदेवे भगवति नान्यद् वेदोद्वहन् रतिम् ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: āste sthāṇurivaikatra divyaṃ varṣaśataṃ sthiraḥ vāsudeve bhagavati nānyad vedodvahan ratim

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: He sat motionless like a post at one and the same spot for a period of a hundred celestial years. As he bore intense love and devotion to Lord Vāsudeva, he ceased to be conscious of anything else (even of his body).

devanagari: स व्यापकतयाऽत्मानं व्यतिरिक्ततयाऽत्मनि । विद्वान्स्वप्न इवामर्श साक्षिणं विरराम ह ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: sa vyāpakatayā vidvānsvapna ivāmarśa sākṣiṇaṃ virarāma ha
translation: [6] He realised within himself, the Self as pervading (and illumining) his own body and mental activities and being distinct and different from them. Just as in a dream, one is witness to (and is therefore different from) mental activities (for example the scene of oneself being beheaded in the dream). And hence he verily ceased to participate (in any activities).
purport:
Footnote [6]:
(i) Malayadhvaja realized Paramātman, the Seer, to be different due to his pervasiveness and his body as ephemeral as a dream and devoted himself solely to Paramātman—Bhāgavata Candrikā
(ii) He reasoned with himself and realized the Supreme Soul as present within his own Self and as all pervasive yet distinct from everything else. Thus renouncing saṃsāra he became liberated while alive (jīvan: muktyavasthām āpa)—Padaratnāvalī
(iii) Due to smaraṇa-bhakti (constant remembrance of God) he felt deep love for the Lord. And he realized him to be shining everywhere in all directions. But feeling him to be distinct and different from himself, he was overcome with the intense feeling of separateness and was fainted in a swoon—Sārārthadarśinī
(iv) He realized his Sell as knower and his body etc. as transient. He became unattached to everything else except his Self.

devanagari: साक्षाद्‍भगवतोक्तेन गुरुणा हरिणा नृप । विशुद्धज्ञानदीपेन स्फुरता विश्वतोमुखम् ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: sākṣād‍bhagavatoktena guruṇā hariṇā nṛpa viśuddhajñānadīpena sphuratā viśvatomukham
translation: [7] Oh King! By the light of pure knowledge which radiates its light in all directions—knowledge which was imparted to him by venerable Hari as his preceptor—he realized himself as within (i.e. identical with) the Supreme Brahman and the Supreme Brahman within himself that way. Ultimately he lost this consciousness (of identity) and (like a fire, fuel of which is burnt down) he automatically ceased to belong to saṃsāra (i.e. became liberated).
purport:
Footnote [7]:
(i) In his religious meditation (upāsana) he realised that his individual Soul (antaryāmin) to be identical with the Supreme Brahman. He continued his upāsaṇā to the end of his life.—Bhāgavata Candrikā
(ii) He set aside the false view about the identity of jīva and Paramātman—Padaratnāvalī
(iii) He realised that the whole—the Supreme Brahman, compriseḍ of the part (the individual Soul) and vice versa. He (thereby) gave up thoughts about Saṃsāra and became liberated—Siddhāntapradīpa
(iv) Oh! How wonderful is the power of Bhakti as Mukti (Liberation) is dependent on Bhakti—Bālaprabodhini

devanagari: परे ब्रह्मणि चात्मानं परं ब्रह्म तथात्मनि । वीक्षमाणो विहायेक्षां अस्माद् उपरराम ह ॥ ४२ ॥
english_devnagari: pare brahmaṇi cātmānaṃ paraṃ brahma tathātmani vīkṣamāṇo vihāyekṣāṃ asmād upararāma ha

translation: [7] Oh King! By the light of pure knowledge which radiates its light in all directions—knowledge which was imparted to him by venerable Hari as his preceptor—he realized himself as within (i.e. identical with) the Supreme Brahman and the Supreme Brahman within himself that way. Ultimately he lost this consciousness (of identity) and (like a fire, fuel of which is burnt down) he automatically ceased to belong to saṃsāra (i.e. became liberated).
purport:
Footnote [7]:
(i) In his religious meditation (upāsana) he realised that his individual Soul (antaryāmin) to be identical with the Supreme Brahman. He continued his upāsaṇā to the end of his life.—Bhāgavata Candrikā
(ii) He set aside the false view about the identity of jīva and Paramātman—Padaratnāvalī
(iii) He realised that the whole—the Supreme Brahman, compriseḍ of the part (the individual Soul) and vice versa. He (thereby) gave up thoughts about Saṃsāra and became liberated—Siddhāntapradīpa
(iv) Oh! How wonderful is the power of Bhakti as Mukti (Liberation) is dependent on Bhakti—Bālaprabodhini

devanagari: पतिं परमधर्मज्ञं वैदर्भी मलयध्वजम् । प्रेम्णा पर्यचरद्धित्वा भोगान् सा पतिदेवता ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: patiṃ paramadharmajñaṃ vaidarbhī malayadhvajam premṇā paryacaraddhitvā bhogān sā patidevatā
translation: Oh King! At that time the Princess of Vidarbha (Malayadhvaja’s queen) who looked upon her husband as a god. renounced all pleasures and affectionately waited upon her husband Malayadhvaja, the knower of the highest form of religion.

devanagari: चीरवासा व्रतक्षामा वेणीभूतशिरोरुहा । बभावुपपतिं शान्ता शिखा शान्तमिवानलम् ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: cīravāsā vratakṣāmā veṇībhūtaśiroruhā babhāvupapatiṃ śāntā śikhā śāntamivānalam

translation: Clad in dark garments, emaciated by observance of vows and with her hair on the head matted, she appeared in the company of her husband like a smokeless flame near flameless fire (of live charcoals).

devanagari: अजानती प्रियतमं यदोपरतमङ्गना । सुस्थिरासनमासाद्य यथापूर्वमुपाचरत् ॥ ४५ ॥
english_devnagari: ajānatī priyatamaṃ yadoparatamaṅganā susthirāsanamāsādya yathāpūrvamupācarat
translation: Not being aware of the expiry of her beloved-most husband who was firm in his sitting posture (even after death), the lady approached him and waited upon him as before.

devanagari: यदा नोपलभेताङ्घ्रौ ऊष्माणं पत्युरर्चती । आसीत् संविग्नहृदया यूथभ्रष्टा मृगी यथा ॥ ४६ ॥
english_devnagari: yadā nopalabhetāṅghrau ūṣmāṇaṃ patyurarcatī āsīt saṃvignahṛdayā yūthabhraṣṭā mṛgī yathā

translation: When, while serving the feet of her husband she did not feel any warmth, therein she got terrified in her heart like a doe which has strayed away from her herd.

devanagari: आत्मानं शोचती दीनं अबन्धुं विक्लवाश्रुभिः । स्तनौ आसिच्य विपिने सुस्वरं प्ररुरोद सा ॥ ४७ ॥
english_devnagari: ātmānaṃ śocatī dīnaṃ abandhuṃ viklavāśrubhiḥ stanau āsicya vipine susvaraṃ praruroda sā

translation: Finding herself miserable and friendless, she was overcome with grief and lamented (her fate). Bathing her breasts in tears, she wept loudly in the jungle.

devanagari: उत्तिष्ठोत्तिष्ठ राजर्षे इमां उदधिमेखलाम् । दस्युभ्यः क्षत्रबन्धुभ्यो बिभ्यतीं पातुमर्हसि ॥ ४८ ॥
english_devnagari: uttiṣṭhottiṣṭha rājarṣe imāṃ udadhimekhalām dasyubhyaḥ kṣatrabandhubhyo bibhyatīṃ pātumarhasi
translation: “Arise, Oh royal sage, arise. You should protect the earth engirdled with seas, as she is afraid of robbers and apostate and wicked Kṣatriyas”

devanagari: एवं विलपन्ती बाला विपिनेऽनुगता पतिम् । पतिता पादयोर्भर्तू रुदत्यश्रूण्यवर्तयत् ॥ ४९ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ vilapantī bālā vipine patitā pādayorbhartū rudatyaśrūṇyavartayat

translation: She piled up a funeral pyre of fuel. Placing her husband’s body on it, she set it on fire. Weeping (all the while) she determined to follow him in death (by burning herself along with his body).

devanagari: तत्र पूर्वतरः कश्चित् सखा ब्राह्मण आत्मवान् । सान्त्वयन्वल्गुना साम्ना तामाह रुदतीं प्रभो ॥ ५१ ॥
english_devnagari: tatra pūrvataraḥ kaścit sakhā brāhmaṇa ātmavān sāntvayanvalgunā sāmnā tāmāha rudatīṃ prabho

translation: (At that time) there appeared an old friend of hers, a Brāhmaṇa who had mastered his Self. Soothing the lady with sweet consoling words, he spoke to the weeping lady (as follows):

devanagari: ब्राह्मण उवाच । का त्वं कस्यासि को वायं शयानो यस्य शोचसि । जानासि किं सखायं मां येनाग्रे विचचर्थ ह ॥ ५२ ॥
english_devnagari: brāhmaṇa uvāca kā tvaṃ kasyāsi ko vāyaṃ śayāno yasya śocasi jānāsi kiṃ sakhāyaṃ māṃ yenāgre vicacartha ha

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
The Brāhmaṇa said:
Who are you? Whose (daughter) are you? Who is this man lying down for whom you are weeping? Don’t you recognize me as your friend with whom you used to roam about formerly (before the creation of the universe)?

devanagari: अपि स्मरसि चात्मानं अविज्ञातसखं सखे । हित्वा मां पदमन्विच्छन् भौमभोगरतो गतः ॥ ५३ ॥
english_devnagari: api smarasi cātmānaṃ avijñātasakhaṃ sakhe hitvā māṃ padamanvicchan bhaumabhogarato gataḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative single from √ man class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 8 verb]
[nominative single from √ man class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ man class 8 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 8 verb]
[nominative single from √ iṣ class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ iṣ class 6 verb]

translation: Friend! (Even if you do not recognize me) do you remember yourself as having a friend called Avijñāta and that you being given to earthly pleasures, left me in search of some place.

devanagari: हंसावहं च त्वं चार्य सखायौ मानसायनौ । अभूतामन्तरा वौकः सहस्रपरिवत्सरान् ॥ ५४ ॥
english_devnagari: haṃsāvahaṃ ca tvaṃ cārya sakhāyau mānasāyanau abhūtāmantarā vaukaḥ sahasraparivatsarān
translation: [8] Oh noble Sir I You and I were swans, friends, living in the Mānasa lake. For a period of one thousand years (while the great deluge lasted), both of us were without any abode.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
(i) Haṃsa means one who has annihilated all agitations or emotions (hinasti nirasyati vikārān iti haṃsaḥ). They are by nature deathless and untouched by pleasures of senses. Enjoyment of pleasure to jīva is due to upādhi (conditioning)—Bhāgavata Candrikā
(ii) Oh learned one! Both of us are haṃsas which are by nature free from all impurities. Our abode is the Mānasa lake (i.e. knowledge). We have lived together for thousands of years. The memory of previous birth is aroused as a result of the accumulated merits.—Padaratnāvalī
(iii) mānasam etc.: Our abode was the heart in the subtle body (sūkṣmaśarīragataṃ hṛdayam).—Siddhāntapradīpa

devanagari: स त्वं विहाय मां बन्धो गतो ग्राम्यमतिर्महीम् । विचरन् पदमद्राक्षीः कयाचिन् निर्मितं स्त्रिया ॥ ५५ ॥
english_devnagari: sa tvaṃ vihāya māṃ bandho gato grāmyamatirmahīm vicaran padamadrākṣīḥ kayācin nirmitaṃ striyā
translation: Oh brother? You whose original nature was that, left me and with your heart set on carnal pleasures, you went down to the earth. While roaming there, you saw a place built by a certain woman (Māyā).

devanagari: पञ्चारामं नवद्वारं एकपालं त्रिकोष्ठकम् । षट्कुलं पञ्चविपणं पञ्चप्रकृति स्त्रीधवम् ॥ ५६ ॥
english_devnagari: pañcārāmaṃ navadvāraṃ ekapālaṃ trikoṣṭhakam ṣaṭkulaṃ pañcavipaṇaṃ pañcaprakṛti strīdhavam
translation: It was provided with five gardens, nine gates, one guard, three surrounding walls, six families (of merchants), five market places. It was constructed with five materials, and was ruled by a woman.

devanagari: पञ्चेन्द्रियार्था आरामा द्वारः प्राणा नव प्रभो । तेजोऽबन्नानि कोष्ठानि कुलमिन्द्रियसङ्ग्रहः ॥ ५७ ॥
english_devnagari: pañcendriyārthā ārāmā dvāraḥ prāṇā nava prabho tejo
translation: The gardens are the objects of senses; the nine gates are the openings (viz. two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, one mouth, the penis and anus) for Prāṇa (who alone works as a guard). Fire, water and food are the surrounding walls, and the group of five sense organs and the mind are the families (of merchants).

devanagari: विपणस्तु क्रियाशक्तिः भूतप्रकृतिरव्यया । शक्त्यधीशः पुमांस्त्वत्र प्रविष्टो नावबुध्यते ॥ ५८ ॥
english_devnagari: vipaṇastu kriyāśaktiḥ bhūtaprakṛtiravyayā śaktyadhīśaḥ pumāṃstvatra praviṣṭo nāvabudhyate

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [instrumental single from √ u class 1 verb], [instrumental single from √ u class 2 verb], [instrumental single from √ u class 5 verb]
[present middle third single], [present passive third single]

translation: The market places were the five conative organs; the five elements constituted the imperishable materials and the power i.e. Intelligence was the ruler. Under her dominance the man who enters this abode loses his Self consciousness in deep sleep.

devanagari: तस्मिंस्त्वं रामया स्पृष्टो रममाणोऽश्रुतस्मृतिः । तत्सङ्गादीदृशीं प्राप्तो दशां पापीयसीं प्रभो ॥ ५९ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmiṃstvaṃ rāmayā spṛṣṭo ramamāṇo tatsaṅgādīdṛśīṃ prāpto daśāṃ pāpīyasīṃ prabho
translation: Being overcome with (the charms of) the woman there, you enjoyed yourself in her company and forgot the memory of the previously known fact (your original status of being the Brahman). It is due to your association with her that you have been reduced to this wretched condition, Oh Lord.

devanagari: न त्वं विदर्भदुहिता नायं वीरः सुहृत्तव । न पतिस्त्वं पुरञ्जन्या रुद्धो नवमुखे यया ॥ ६० ॥
english_devnagari: na tvaṃ vidarbhaduhitā nāyaṃ vīraḥ suhṛttava na patistvaṃ purañjanyā ruddho navamukhe yayā
translation: You are not the daughter of the king of Vidarbha. Nor is this warrior your friend. Nor are you the husband of Purāñjanī by whom you were interned in the city of nine gates.

devanagari: माया ह्येषा मया सृष्टा यत्पुमांसं स्त्रियं सतीम् । मन्यसे नोभयं यद्वै हंसौ पश्यावयोर्गतिम् ॥ ६१ ॥
english_devnagari: māyā hyeṣā mayā sṛṣṭā yatpumāṃsaṃ striyaṃ satīm manyase nobhayaṃ yadvai haṃsau paśyāvayorgatim

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ sṛj class 6 verb], [vocative plural from √ sṛj class 6 verb]

[present middle second single], [present passive second single]
[nominative dual from √ paś class 10 verb], [vocative dual from √ paś class 10 verb], [accusative dual from √ paś class 10 verb]

translation: It is really the illusion created by me that you regard the man (Purañjana in the previous birth) as the virtuous woman—(Vaidarbhī in this birth). You are neither. We are haṃsas (pure spirits). Please see (realize) our real nature (course).

devanagari: अहं भवान्न चान्यस्त्वं त्वमेवाहं विचक्ष्व भोः । न नौ पश्यन्ति कवयः छिद्रं जातु मनागपि ॥ ६२ ॥
english_devnagari: ahaṃ bhavānna cānyastvaṃ tvamevāhaṃ vicakṣva bhoḥ na nau paśyanti kavayaḥ chidraṃ jātu manāgapi
translation: I am identical with you. You are not other than me. Please note that you and I are one. Wise men never see even the slightest difference or distinction between us.

devanagari: यथा पुरुष आत्मानं एकं आदर्शचक्षुषोः । द्विधाभूतमवेक्षेत तथैवान्तरमावयोः ॥ ६३ ॥
english_devnagari: yathā puruṣa ātmānaṃ ekaṃ ādarśacakṣuṣoḥ dvidhābhūtamavekṣeta tathaivāntaramāvayoḥ
translation: Just as a man finds his one Self as divided in two in (his reflection in) the mirror and in the pupils of eyes, similar is the difference between us two.

devanagari: एवं स मानसो हंसो हंसेन प्रतिबोधितः । स्वस्थस्तद्व्यभिचारेण नष्टामाप पुनः स्मृतिम् ॥ ६४ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ sa mānaso haṃso haṃsena pratibodhitaḥ svasthastadvyabhicāreṇa naṣṭāmāpa punaḥ smṛtim

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative single from √ naś class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ naś class 4 verb]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]

translation: In this way, the haṃsa (swan) from the Mānasa lake i.e. Jīva who was awakened (was made to realize his original state) by his fellow swan, became established in his own real nature. He regained the memory of his original nature which was lost due to his separation from him.

devanagari: बर्हिष्मन्नेतदध्यात्मं पारोक्ष्येण प्रदर्शितम् । यत्परोक्षप्रियो देवो भगवान्विश्वभावनः ॥ ६५ ॥
english_devnagari: barhiṣmannetadadhyātmaṃ pārokṣyeṇa pradarśitam yatparokṣapriyo devo bhagavānviśvabhāvanaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation:
Oh Barhiṣman! This knowledge about the Self has been explained to you in an indirect manner; for the venerable Lord, the creator of the universe, likes to remain beyond the ken of senses.
